2B2C -1A2C -4B2C -2B3. Meanings of the individual divisions  
2B2C -1A2C -4B2C -2B1. Actual meaning  
There are two: concentrations and formless absorptions. With respect to the first, 
concentrations, there are two types by way of the particularities of ca usal absorption and 
resultant rebirth.  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
192  The first [a concentration that is a causal absorption] is concomitant with a single -pointed 
mind that has transcended the lower levels by means of branches. 
 The second [a concentration that is a resultant rebirth]  is a fruition of having cultivated 
the concentration that is a causal absorption in another rebirth, and is a result concordant with the cause. It is a  propert y attained from birth in the continuum of sentient beings of 
Brahma Type
107 and so forth until Akanistha108. 
 Also, with respect to the formless absorptions, a formless absorption that is a causal 
absorption is concomitant with a single -pointed mind that has transcended the lower levels 
by means of the object of observation. A formless absorption that is  a resultant rebirth is 
either a fruition of having cultivated that in another rebirth, or a result concordant with the 
cause. It is a  propert y attained from birth in the continuum of sentient beings who are born 
in the four types of formless [realms].  
 Because the concentrations definitely arise from the purification of the coarse branches of 
the lower levels by the higher levels, there are the particularities of dissimilar branches, whereby they are said to “transcend by way of branches.” The formless [ab sorptions] do not 
have dissimilar branches; because they definitely arise from the lower levels by means of 
dissimilar objects of observation, they are said to “transcend by way of objects of 
observation.”  
2B2C -1A2C -4B2C -2B2. Divisions 
With regard  to the c oncentrations, there are four —from the first to the fourth. With respect 
to the formless absorptions, there are four— the source Infinite Space and so forth. Further, 
the four concentrations and the first three formless absorptions are each of three types —
afflicted, pure and uncontaminated. The Peak of Existence has the two former [types]; 
according to the common [ vehicles ], there are no uncontaminated ones, because since its 
object of observation and aspect are unclear, it is unsuitable as a support of supramundane 
paths which are very clear.   
 That bodhisattvas  are supported on a desire realm mind and the Peak of Existence by way 
of skillful  means has also been explained by the Acarya. A preparation of the first 
concentration is of two —pure and uncontamina ted. The rest are only of pure ones. There are 
no afflicted [preparations], because they would be  unable to act to separate from the 
attachment to the lower levels.  
 There are uncontaminated ones with respect to a preparation of the first concentration, 
because stream -enterers and so forth who have not attained an actual concentration generate 
a supramundane path only in dependence on a preparation of the first concentration; an 
uncontaminated path does not need to be supported on the remaining preparations . 
 It is unnecessary to act to separate from the attachment to the lower levels by way of 
actual [concentrations], because they have already been separated from by way of the 
preparations. Contaminated actual [concentrations] do not act as antidotes for th eir own 
level and higher levels. The uncontaminated ones act as antidotes for their own level and higher levels, whereby they are paths that separate from attachment, manifestly achieve 
excellent qualities and are also paths of  abiding happily in the present life.  
                                             
 
107 Brahma Type is the first of seventeen levels of the form realm.  
108 Akanistha is the last of  seventeen levels of the form realm.  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
193 2B2C -1A2C -4B2C -2B3. Meanings of the individual divisions  
The first concentration is of two types:  
(1) a mere actual concentration  
(2) a special actual concentration  
 
The first [a mere first actual concentration] is (i) that concomitant with a single -pointed 
mind distinguished by having separated from the attachment to the desire realm through the antidotal branches of investigation and analysis, (ii) which possesses the benefit branch of 
joy and happiness generated from isola tion.  
 The second [a special first actual concentration] is (i) that concomitant with a single -
pointed mind distinguished by having separated even from the attachment to investigation 
through the antidotal branch of analysis, (ii) which possesses equanimi ty feeling. The 
second concentration and above do not have the two, mere and special, because, without 
separating from the attachment to their own level, they are unable to act to separate from the 
attachment to some  branch of their own level. 
 The second concentration acts to separate from the attachment to investigation and 
analysis through the antidotal branch of internal clarity, and possesses the benefit branch of 
joy and happiness generated from meditative stabilization.  
 The third concentration is distinguished by having separated from attachment to joy 
through the antidotal branches of mindfulness, introspection, and equanimity, and possesses 
the benefit branch of happiness that  is isolated from joy.  
 The fourth concentration is distinguished by having separated from attachment to 
happiness through the antidotal branch es of completely pure mindfulness and equanimity, 
and possesses the benefit branch of equanimity feeling.  
 With respect to the four formless absorptions, t he source Infinite Space, having seen the 
discrimination of form s and obstruct ivenes s as coarse, repeatedly destroys this 
discrimination and repeatedly familiarizes with the discrimination that thinks “Space is 
infinite,” whereby it is concomitant with a s ingle -pointed mind in which that is manifest at 
the time of the actual [absorption].  
 The source Infinite Consciousness, having viewed that very attention thinking “Space is 
infinite” as an object to be ceased, by taking to mind the thought, “That very attention is 
infinite,” manifests it.   
 The source Nothingness, by taking to mind the thought, “There is no object to be 
apprehended below that,” manifests it.  
 With respect to the source Not Without Discrimination, discrimination refers to disease, 
boils, and pain. Without discrimination refers to thorough confusion. 
 Without Discrimination Not Without Discrimination, by taking to mind the thought, 
“Peace is sublime,” is that concomitant with a single -pointed mind that is able to set in 
equipoise on that. 
 Regarding the afflicted concentrations:  
• there is a predominance of craving that relishes the taste of happiness;  
• there is a predominance of view that acts to generate bad views such as the view of a limit to the past  
• there is a predominance of pride that i s a mind that is puffed up due to [awareness of 
having achieved] a concentration;  
• there is a predominance of doubt thinking, “Is that which has been attained by me a path to liberation or not?”  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
194  
A pure one is that concomitant with a single -pointed mind t hat is not polluted by the 
afflictions of its own level. It is of four types:  (1) partial concordance with degeneration  
(2) partial concordance with abidance  
(3) partial concordance with enhancement  
(4) partial concordance with definite discrimination  
 
Further, these are [respectively]  pure concentrations which:  
(1) is concordant with the generation of afflictions of its own or a lower level [immediately] 
afterwards  
(2) induces another pure level of the same level as itself [immediately] afterwards  
(3) in duces a pure one of a higher level [immediately] afterwards  
(4) induces a supramundane path [immediately] afterwards  
 Know the others similarly. The Peak of Existence has no partial concordance with 
enhancement, because there is no other level higher than  it. According to the common 
[presentation ], it also has no partial concordance with definite discrimination, because an 
arya who has attained a mind of the Peak of Existence generates an uncontaminated path by 
actualizing the mind of Nothingness.  
 
The fi rst concentration has five branches:  
(1) investigation  
(2) analysis  
(3-4) happiness and joy generated from isolation  
(5) single -pointed mind  
 
The second concentration has four branches:  
(1) internal clarity  
(2-3) joy and happiness generated from meditative  stabilization  
(4) single -pointed mind  
 
The third concentration has five branches:  
(1) mindfulness  
(2) introspection  
(3) equanimity  
(4) happiness  
(5) single -pointed mind  
  
The fourth concentration has four branches:  
(1-2) completely pure mindfulness and equanimity  
(3) equanimity feeling  
(4) single -pointed mind  
 
The branches are presented as four, five, and so forth in terms of:  
• the antidotal branches which abandon harm, 
• the benefit branches which achieve benefit, and  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
195 • the branches of abidance which are bo th of them.  
 
Special insight  exists in the formless absorptions, but calm abiding is predominant and they 
are of the same taste in the state of that, thereby the branches are not presented differently.  
 Those who have attained a concentration seek definite  emergence  from coarse and subtle  
manifest feelings  of happiness and suffering ; they do not seek definite emergence  from  
equanimity, because it is difficult for heedless ordinary beings to realize that compositional 
suffering is in the nature of suffering . 
 The four concentrations are presented [respectively] by way of the stages of acting to 
separate from attachment to:  
• mental unhappiness  that are coarse sufferin g feelings ;  
• negative  states that are  subtle suffering feeling s;  
• mental happiness that are coarse happy feelings ; and  
• the power of subtle happiness .   
 
The four formless absorptions are presented [respectively] by way of the stages of definite 
emergenc e from the four:  
• with respect to the first of the two —the discrimination of outer forms and th e 
apprehension of inner primary consciousnesses as beautiful (from among the four coarse 
and subtle [objects] with respect to manifest contaminated discriminations)—[the 
discrimination of outer forms]:  
(1) the discrimination of forms and obstructi veness , the object of abandonment  
(2) the discrimination of Infinite Space, the antidote, and  
• with respect to [the second of the two,] the apprehension of primary consciousnesses as 
beautiful:  
(3) adherence to primary consciousnesses, the discordant class and  
(4) the discrimination of Nothingness, the antidote  
2B2C -1A2C -4B2C -2C. Result  
Know that from cultivating the small, middl ing, and great levels of the first concentration, 
one will accomplish the rebirths of the concentrations of the three other abodes  of th e first 
concentration; by cultivating the small, middle, and great levels of the second concentration, 
third concentration and fourth concentration, one will accomplish the rebirths of the 
concentrations of Little Light, and so forth. By cultivating the fo rmless absorptions one will 
accomplish the aggregates of the four names [feeling, discrimination, compositional factors and consciousness es] at that location where one dies and transmigrates.  
 Know that although [the formless realms] do not have divisions  of higher or lower levels  
that are “other abodes ,” due to cultivating small, middle, and great levels, one will 
accomplish high or low rebirths, and bad or good rebirths
 of the formless [realms].  
 The small level is such that neither of the two, continuou s application and respectful 
application is strong. The middle level is strong in either one. The great level is strong in 
both. “High or low rebirths, and bad or good rebirths ” [of the formless realms] refer to long 
or short life span, many or few afflict ions, and so forth.  
 The four immeasurables are easy to understand, and the divisions of great compassion 
will be explained. If you wish to generate them in the continuum by means of the stages of these concentrations, you should know them from explanatio ns elsewhere.

EXPLANATION  
Know that as the stages of the achievings of accumulation. What are they ? The y are the 
seventeen, loving- kindness  and so forth , bearing the characteristics  of: 
(1-7) behavior together with support ;  
(8-11) meditative equipoise and sub sequent attainment ;  
(12-13) the divisions of the two accumulations ;  
(14-15) achieving the result and holding the object of observation ; and   
(16-17) acting as a support for excellent qualities and abandoning obscurations .

COMMENTARY 
So the root text is said. By not observing (1) great compassion, (2) generosity, (3) ethics, (4) 
patience, (5) joyous effort, (6) concentration, (7) wisdom, (8 -10) the paths of calm abiding , 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
197 special insight , and the union, (11) skillful  means, (12) exalted  wisdom, (13) merit, (14) the 
paths of seeing and so forth, (15) the retentions (dharanis) of words and so forth, (16) the 
grounds, and (17) the antidotes, since one achieves  all the aims of the Mahayana without 
passing beyond the conventional truth, these , compassion and so forth, by way of entities of 
perfect achievement, because of issuing forth great enlightenment, since great compassion 
and so forth are the accumulations, the achieving of accumulation is of seventeen types.  
 
EXPLANATION  
Following the a chieving of armor, s utra says: “With respect to the ten, beginning from 
loving- kindness until union [1- 10], ‘donning of the great armor’ is expressed. With just 
what is a great bodhisattva said to ‘don the great armor?’” The repl ies were indicated by 
means  of the reason for engaging the term “armor.”   
 With respect to the seven, skillful  means and below [11- 17], following the achieving of 
engagement, [sutra says], “Bhagavan, what is the great vehicle of a great bodhisattva? With 
just what is one considered  to have correctly entered the great vehicle of a great 
bodhisattva? What gave rise to the vehicle? Where will one abide by way of the vehicle? What will arise by way of the vehicle?” The repl ies were indicated by means of closely 
indicating the answer s to the five questions in terms of the training in wisdom and 
compassion, the two not being mutually different, transcending the discordant classes, the 
occasion being definite, pliancy in the application of the antidotal classes, and the realizer.  
 Regarding  those [11 -17], the five from skillful  means until the retentions [11 -15] are 
taught by indicating the entity of the Mahayana in the reply to the first question. The 
accumulation of grounds [16] is taught by way of the reply to the second question. The 
accumulation of antidotes [17] is taught by way of the reply to the questions at the end.  
 The seven accumulations included within the accumulations of perfect attitude and 
application are as follows . A bodhisattva —who has donned the armor in the practice 
possessing the six perfections —cultivates (1) the great compassion  which wishes to free all 
trainees from suffering at the beginning.  
 Further, that which, by observing mere sentient beings, bears the aspect of wishing them 
to be separated from suffering is the compassion observing sentient beings. The attribute  of 
aspect is similar; that which observes sentient beings qualified by the dawning of emptiness 
of coarse selflessness, and that which observes sentient beings qualified by the dawning of 
freedom from  elaboration are [respectively] the compassion observing phenomena and the 
compassion of non- observation.  
 Someone who asserts that the last compassion [the compassion of non -observation] 
realizes emptiness is unskilled in the object of observation and asp ect of compassion. Those 
who are anguished due to compassion take care of others —through accomplishing the 
happiness of this life by way of (2) generosity , accomplishing the happiness of high status 
of the next rebirth by way of (3) ethics , accomplishing p recise definite happiness by means 
of setting them in the happiness of meditative stabilization by way of (4)  patience , (5) 
joyous effort and (6) concentration , and accomplishing everlasting happiness by setting 
them in nirvana by way of (7) wisdom .  
 Rega rding the four included within meditative equipoise and subsequent attainment, 
meditative equipoise is indicate d as (8-10) the accumulation of the paths of calm abiding , 
special insight and the union of calm abiding and special insight . Its meaning, according to 
the explanation in [Haribhadra’s] Great Commentary, is as follows:  
 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
198 QUALM: The  Compendium of Doctrine Sutra  (Dharmasamgitisutra) says, “By 
setting the mind in meditative equipoise  one will excellently know  exactly  and 
perfectly .” The meditative equipoise taught is calm abiding , and knowing exactly 
is special insight . Calm abiding is abidance without movement. Special insight  is 
the awareness analyzing the meaning. Therefore, are the two not discordant? 
How will there be a union?  
 At the time when the mind has become firm with respect to the object of observation of 
calm abiding , stemming from analysis with wisdom, one will generate the illumination of 
the exalted wisdom realizing the perfect meaning. Like the dispelling of darkne ss by the 
illumination generated, the obscurations are abandoned; if it were not of one entity with 
calm abiding , it would not be able to abandon all the manifest objects of abandonment 
without exception. Hence, calm abiding and special insight  engage the object like the eye 
and illumination. In order to generate the perfect exalted wisdom, they abide concordantly; it is not that they are discordant like illumination and darkness. 
 Possessing the accumulation of the union [of calm abiding and special insigh t], one will 
come to possess (11) the accumulation of skillful means  that enacts others’ welfare through 
the attention to omniscience, and (12-13) the accumulation of exalted wisdom  and merit 
from having familiarized with such paths of meditative equipoise  and subsequent 
attainment. Through having familiarized with the two accumulations, one will come to 
possess (14) the accumulation of the paths of seeing and so forth . By possessing the 
accumulation of paths, one will attain (15) the accumulation of the retention of words and 
so forth  in which one is able to remember without limit words and meanings for many eons 
without forgetting them. What is the entity of retention? It is mindfulness and wisdom 
concomitant with calm abiding . In regard  to secret mantra , this is a case of the name of a 
result imputed to its cause. When divided, there are the retention of forbearance, the retention of secret mantra, and the retention s of words and meanings. 
 The first [the retention of forbearance] is as follows. Having a nalyzed the meaning of the 
forty -two syllables, A RA PA TSA NA and so forth. One meditates on the meaning of 
emptiness, the mode of abidance of the syllables, thinking, “The letter A is the door to all phenomena, because it is not produced,” whereby one ge nerates a meditative stabilization 
and wisdom which is not afraid of it; this is the attainment of the retention of forbearance. It exists from the forbearance level of the path of preparation.  
 The second [the retention of secret mantra] is as follows. On e attains the retention of 
secret mantra through being able  to compose secret mantras that are able to protect others, 
such as those that pacify negativities through their power. It exists from the forbearance level of the path of preparation , and the spec ial, from having attained the path of seeing.   
 The last , the two retentions [of words and meanings] are the ability to retain the words 
and meanings of phenomena for eons without forgetting them, and exist from having attained the forbearance level of th e path of preparation and above.  
 There are (16)  the accumulation of the  grounds , by way of acting as the support of 
excellent qualities, and (17)  the accumulation of the antidotes , for the sake of abandoning 
the discordant classes.  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3A -3B. Rea son for positing them as accumulations  
The subject —the seventeen, great compassion and so forth—are the accumulation of 
causes issuing forth great enlightenment, because  they hold the result of great 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
199 enlightenment by way of entities of perfect quick achiev ement through these seventeen 
accumulations , compassion and so forth. They hold the result of final great enlightenment, 
since, by realizing them without observing them  to be truly existent, without passing 
beyond ultimate truth, one achieves all the aims of  the stages of the Mahayana path that is 
included within the extensive, without passing beyond the conventional truth.109 In brief, 
these seventeen include all the stages of the profound and extensive paths that are objects to 
be practiced by bodhisattvas.  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3A -3C. Definite enumeration  
The achieving of accumulation is of seventeen types. These seventeen achievings of 
accumulation are the accumulations of causes that are free from the faults of being too many 
or too few for producing omniscience.

COMMENTARY 
With regard to that, the accum ulation of exalted wisdom: (1) the inner, (2) the outer, (3) 
both, (4) the empty, (5) the great , (6) the ultimate, (7) the compounded, (8) the 
uncompounded, (9) that passed beyond extremes, (10) that without beginning or end, (11) 
that not discarded, (12) nature, (13) all phenomena, (14) the specifically -characterized, (15) 
that not observed, (16) the entitiness of non -things, (17) things, (18) non -things, (19) self - 
entity, and (20) others ’ entity —by particularities of emptiness, twenty types.  
 I declare  here:  
 
Inner and outer,  
Both and things that  are empty,  
Directions, paths of nirvana,  
Produced from conditions and the other,  
Passed beyond extremes, without beginning, not discarded,  
Nature, all phenomena,  
Phenomena that are  produced and so forth, the past and so forth,  
That possessing and entity of the other,  
Space and that called “empty” and  
Entitiness of an other that is a phenomenon:  
Since they are empty of their own entity,  Emptiness is asserted as twenty.   
                                             
 
109 To appreciate this passage, one must understand the etymology of “sambhara,” the Sanskrit word that is 
translated as “accumulation” here. See Jetsun Chokyi Gyeltsen’s Ocean of Sport . 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
200  
EXPLANATION  
Twenty emptinesses are declared  here. (1) The subject —the six inner  sources —are 
devoid of  true existence, because they are free from being truly existent one or many. Apply 
the sign and the predicate similarly to the rest. (2 -3) The emptiness of true existence of the 
six outer sources, and of the support of the powers due to being both [outer and inner] 
relative to the material and the sources are the emptiness of the outer  and the emptiness of 
both the outer and the inner. (4) The emptiness of true existence of things that are empty  
of true existence is the emptiness of the  just empty .110 (5) The emptiness of true existence 
of the ten directions  is the emptiness of the  great. (6) The emptiness of true  existence of 
nirvana is the emptiness of the ultimate . They [5 & 6] negate cyclic existence and peace as 
truly  existent. 
 (7-8) The emptiness of true existence of the compounded produced from conditions  and 
the emptiness of true existence of the other , the uncompounded , such as space, are the 
emptiness es of the compounded and the uncompounded . (9) T he emptiness of true 
existence of the  past, future and  middle , which are empty of the extremes of permanence 
and severance , is the emptiness of that passed beyo nd extremes . (10-11) The emptiness es 
of true existence of the beginning and the end, and of what is to be adopted [not]  discarded 
are [respectively] the emptiness of that without beginning or end  and the emptiness of 
that not discarded.  Alternatively, for the latter [the emptiness of that not discard ed], take 
generosity and so forth that are not to be discarded as the bas es of emptiness .  
 (12-13) The emptinesses of true existence of the mode of abidance and of all phenomena 
which are ex pressions [of the mode of abidance] are [respectively] the emptinesses of 
nature  and of all phenomena . (14 -15) The emptinesses of true existence of phenomena 
that are produced and so forth , [and] of the past and so forth are [respectively] the 
emptinesses of the specifically -characterized , and of that not observed. (16 -17) The 
emptinesses of true existence of th at possessing causes and conditions and that  which  is a 
composite, and of the entity of the other , the five aggregates , are [respectively] the 
emptinesses of the entitiness of non -things , and of things . (18-19) The emptinesses of true 
existence of uncompounded  space and of that called “empty nature ” are [respectively] the 
emptinesses of non- things , and of self- entity . (20) The emptiness of true existence of the 
entitiness of another that is a phenomenon, the emptiness that is the  emptiness of another 
agent, is the emptiness of the  others ’ entity. In this way, since the twenty bases of 
emptiness are empty of an entity  that is their own true exi stence , emptiness is asserted as 
twenty.   
 This is an extensive division; when condensed, the first sixteen can be subsumed under 
the last four. Those [ in which non -thusness]  has been taken as the bases of emptiness , the 
property -possessors,  can be subsum ed under the first two [17 & 18]. Those in which 
thusness has been taken as the bases of emptiness can be subsumed under the last two [19 & 
20]. According to those who assert that the basis of emptiness of the emptiness of the just 
empty is an exalted wisd om, there are three [ways] in which thusness is taken as the bases 
of emptiness: refuting the qualm, “Has the mode of existence of phenomena been newly produced by the aryas through knowing and seeing?” refers to the emptiness of nature; 
                                             
 
110 For a more common presention of this fourth emptiness, see the first type of emptiness discussed under 
the outline 2B2C -2A1B -1A1B -2C2D -2. Aspect of pacification . 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
201 refut ation of the qualms, “Is its own entity truly existent?” and “Has it been created by an 
agent that is a different object?” refers  to the last two emptiness es.  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -1B. The object -possessor s, the twenty exalted wisdoms that  realize those  
The accumulation of exalted wisdom is included within the levels of ordinary beings, the 
ten grounds, and the exalted wisdom of a buddha. Regarding the first [the accumulation of 
exalted wisdom included within the levels of ordinary beings], the three exal ted wisdoms 
that realize (1 -3) the emptiness of the inner, the outer, and that which is  both of them stop 
the adherence to true existence of the three —the utilizers, the objects of utilization and their 
supports —and are posited on the occasion of the path of accumulation. Regarding the 
teaching in [Haribhadra’s] Great Commentary, “By way of utilization,” if one were to explain that the first three [exalted wisdoms are posited on the occasions of] the first three 
levels of the path of preparation, and the fo urth on the supreme mundane quality, it appears 
to be just suitable. However, if we were to posit the first three on the occasions of the path 
of accumulation, there would not be any contradiction. (4) The realization of emptiness as 
just empty by a mundane wisdom arisen from meditation is posited on the path of 
preparation.   
 (5-6) The emptiness of the great and the emptiness of the ultimate impel the exalted 
wisdoms directly realizing them to realize that dharmadhatu is omnipresent in property -
possessors, and to realize that dharmadhatu is the supreme meaning. Hence these are 
respectively posited on the first two grounds. 
 (7) The emptiness of the compounded impels the exalted wisdom realizing it to realize 
that dharmadhatu is a meaning concordant with th e cause. (8) The emptiness of the 
uncompounded impels the exalted wisdom realizing it to realize that dharmadhatu is a 
meaning that cannot be fully held. These two are respectively posited on the third and fourth 
grounds.  
 (9-10) The emptinesses of that pa ssed beyond extremes, and that without beginning or 
end, impel the realization that dharmadhatu is not a different continuum and the realization 
of the meaning of non -existence of the thoroughly afflicted and the completely pure as 
empty. Hence they are po sited on the fifth and sixth grounds.  
 Likewise, the exalted wisdom that is the object -possessor impelled by (11) the emptiness 
of that not discarded is posited on the seventh ground, and the object- possessors impelled by 
(12-13) the emptinesses of nature and of all phenomena are posited on the eighth ground.  
 The exalted wisdoms impelled by (14 -15) the emptinesses of the specifically -
characterized and of that not observed are posited on the ninth ground. The exalted wisdoms 
that are the object- possessors impelled by (16 -17) the emptiness of the entitiness of non -
things and [the emptiness] of things are posited on the tenth ground. The exalted wisdoms 
that are the object -possessors impelled by (18 -20) the emptinesses of non-things, of one's 
own entity, and of the  entity of others are posited on the buddha ground.  
 The last three are supports that have exhaustively abandoned the two obscurations as well 
as their latencies, and are posited on the buddha ground by way of the meaning of being self-arisen; the ex alted wisdoms impelled by the first seventeen [have abandoned] some 
manifest afflictions or seeds that are objects of abandonment, and are posited on the tenth ground and below. As explained above, the emptiness of the great impel the realization that 
dharmadhatu is a meaning that is omnipresent. Although it has been explained that it is 
posited on the first ground, I think there is no contradiction. Further, [Haribhadra’s] Great 
Commentary says:  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
202  
Regarding that, at that time, by meditating, thinking, ‘All  phenomena are 
selfless,’ although that has already realized dharmadhatu by way of all natures 
due to [its] partlessness, in reliance upon generating certainty, he individually 
realizes it by way of a meaning that is omnipresent. Hence he will generate a 
very clear knower that acts directly on the absence  of entity of all phenomena. At 
that time, by perfectly entering into the faultlessness of bodhisattvas, he will attain the path of seeing.  
 The meaning of that explanation is as follows. The teaching that  dharmadhatu is partless 
[means]  that it does not appear to a direct cognizer as individual parts that are dissimilar 
aspects like blue and yellow; it is not that it is inappropriate to divide it into parts that are isolates and so forth. For, there are as many parts as there are property -possessors.  
 Since the dharmata of a sentient being and the dharmata of a buddha do not differ even 
slightly as to whether or not they are realized through direct cognition by the meditative 
equipoise of a learner arya, asc ertainment regarding the dharmata of a buddha is also 
induced by the force of experience. The explanation here that ascertainment is generated progressively means the following. In dependence on the meditative equipoises of the ten 
grounds having abandoned successively the seeds of the two obscurations, great or small 
ascertainment and dissimilar ascertainments are generated regarding the various attributes of 
illusory dependent -arisings during subsequent attainment; the appearances of wrong classes 
[contra dicting] dependent -arisings appearing as illusions during subsequent attainment 
become weaker and weaker.   
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -1C. Analyzing what the objects of observation of the exalted wisdoms are  
[Haribhadra’s] Great Commentary  says:  
 
QUALM: Also, what are the objects of observation of this non - conceptual 
exalted wisdom? …  
 
When its meaning is explained slightly, there are five [divisions]:  
(1) Setting forth the two systems  
(2) Proving the latter as correct  
(3) Rejecting the arguments of others with respect to that [latter system] (4) Refuting the former system  
(5) The reason for not mentioning other divisions of exalted wisdoms  
 
(1) Setting forth the two systems  
The Proponents  of Cognition -Only  assert that the very truly existent [phenomenon] that is  
the emptiness of other- powered phenomena’s emptiness of apprehended -objects and their 
apprehender -subjects being different substances is the object of comprehension of an arya’s 
meditative equipoise. The others assert that [an arya’s meditative equipoise]  realizes that all 
phenomena are empty of true existence. The latter is correct.  
 
(2) Proving the latter as correct  
If it is the object of knowledge of any knower, it is pervaded by being empty of true 
existence , for example, just like the appearance of a dream horse or elephant. Stating that 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
203 this non -conceptual exalted wisdom also “is a knower” is a nature sign. [Dharmakirti’s] 
Ascertainment of Valid Cognition (Pramanav iniscaya ) says: 
 
If the object of observation is not directly cognized, seeing of the me aning will 
not be strongly established.  
 
Therefore, if the object that is the object of observation has not been observed, then the 
object is not realized.  
 Hence it is not that the property of the subject is not established; and since the concordant 
class is seen, it is not a contradictory [sign]. The existence of truly existent objects of 
knowledge will be refuted on the occasion of “No basis ; no going; no production,” [4.30d]. 
It is also not an indeterminate [sign].   
 
(3) Rejecting the arguments of others  
 
PROPONENTS OF MIND -ONLY : The subject —also the emptiness of true existence of all 
phenomena —it follows that it is empty of true existence, because it is an object of 
observation; for example, as in the case of two moons. If you accept this, then it follows that 
all phenomena would be truly existent, because the emptiness of true existence of all phenomena which is asserted by you to be their mode of abidance is also empty of true 
existence.  
 
RESPONSE: There is no fault because, even though it would be appropriate for Devadatta 
to arise again after having been killed if the victim Devadatta and the killer Yaj ñadatta  
themselves were a part of the killing of Devadatta, since the killer itself is not a part of the 
victim and killing, Devadatta would not arise again. Likewise, the very extreme of superimposition, that the emptiness of true existence , (which is similar to the killer,  the 
emptiness of true existence of all phenomena which is asserted by you to be an extreme of deprecation ,) is truly existent  is refuted by the reasoning that refutes the emptiness of true 
existence as truly existent. Hence it follows that it is not necessary to assert again the true existence of all phenomena, the object of negation, because true existence is not any part 
that is of one nature with the pervader of that emptiness of true existence. 
 Furthermore, it follows that it would be inappropriate to express the fault, “If it is an 
ultimate truth, it must be established ultimately; it is contradictory for it to exist 
conven tionally,” because it is inappropriate for the object of observation of meditative 
equipoise to exist ultimately. That follows, because it bears the finality supported upon that 
illusory falsity which is established by a direct valid cognition of conventio nalities, the 
support that is the property -possessor, and because it is contradictory for it to be supported 
in the nature of false true existence.   
(4) Refuting the former system  
 
If you assert that truly existent emptiness is the observed object, then i t follows that —(1) a 
non-conceptual exalted wisdom and (2) the emptiness of apprehended -objects and their 
apprehender -subjects being different substances —would either be one substance or different 
substances, because, since even emptiness exists truly, you  must assert it [emptiness] to be a 
functioning thing. If you accept this, then you must assert that, if they are different 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
204 substances, then [emptiness]  would be  a different substance from other- powered natures. 
Hence that exalted wisdom would be the posse ssor of an object of utilization that is  a stand -
alone emptiness that is unrelated to other- powered natures, whereby it would not eliminate  
erroneous superimposition s regarding other- powered natures. If they were one substance, 
then it follows that emptine ss would be compounded. If you accept that, then meditating on 
it through observing it would not be able to abandon the seeds of the obscurations.  
 Anyone who asserts that it is the Acarya’s assertion that an arya’s meditative equipoise 
explicitly realize s illusory false dependent -arisings clearly indicates that he has not trained 
well in either of the systems of the Consequentialist and the Autonomist Middle -Wayers, 
has explained the Acarya’s assertions wrongly, and that even he himself does not know the 
presentation that protects the view. 
 (5) The reason for not mentioning other divisions of exalted wisdoms  
 Other divisions such as the mirror- like exalted wisdom have not been explained here, 
because I wish to indicate a mere enumeration of the generality which are the divisions that are instances of the accumulations of exalted wisdoms that explicitly observe emptiness, 
and, since there are infinite other divisions that are instances of exalted wisdom, 
[mentioning them here] would become a hindrance to ascertaining the contextual meaning 
of the clear realizations.  
 Although the Proponents of Mind- Only  verbally  assert that emptiness is truly existent, 
some assert that ultimate truth is a permanent thing that does not rely upon eliminating an 
object of negation. You should understand that there exist differences between Mount Meru 
and a mustard seed or between th e sky and the palm of one’s hand. Realize them from the 
extensive explanation in [Gyaltsab’s ] Explanation of the  Mahayana Treatise on the Sublime 
Continuum . 
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2. Particular explanation of the accumulation of grounds  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2A. Meaning of the generality 
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B. Meaning of the branches

2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B-5. Meaning of the name  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B-1. Entity  
This very non- conceptual exalted wisdom, the meditative equipoise directly observing 
thusness that is fully conjoined with great compassion and so forth is called ‘ground’ 
because it is like the ground  since it acts as an abode or a support of excellent qualities. 
Hence it is termed that way.  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B-2. Divisions  
There are ten grounds by way of earlier and later stages which are differentiated by the individual stages that are factors such as  the ability to eliminate the objects of abandonment.  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
205 2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B-3. Attributes 
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2A3A. Attributes of entity  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2A3B. Attributes of the objects of abandonment  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2A3A. Attributes of entity  
On the first ground, th ere are twelve sets of a hundred excellent qualities . 
In one instant :  
(1) one sees a hundred buddhas  
(2) one knows that one is blessed by them  
(3) one enters a hundred meditative stabilizations  
(4) one moves a hundred world systems  
(5) one goes to a hundred world systems  
(6) one causes a hundred world systems to appear  
(7) one ripens a hundred sentient beings  
(8) one abides for a hundred eons  
(9) one en gages  their past and future limits  
(10 one opens a hundred Dharma doors  
(11) one displays a hundred bodies 
(12) one displays a retinue of a hundred bodhisattvas for each body, and so forth  
 
The number of excellent qualities increase s more and more; for example, on the second 
ground, there are twelve sets of a thousand of those excellent qualities. The power to purify stains and the power to traverse the paths also increase. One attains the attribute of 
surpassing perfections, for example, the surpassing perfection of generosity is attained on 
the first ground. One’s fruitional rebirths, such as birth as  a king also increase; on the first 
ground one has control over Jambudvipa, while on the second ground one has control over the four continents.  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2A3B. Attributes of the objects of abandonment  
On the occasion of the path of seeing, the seeds of the 112 acquired afflictive obscurations included within the levels of the three realms and the seeds of the 108 acquired knowledge 
obscurations are destroyed. Know that the seeds of the sixteen  afflictions that are objects to 
be abandoned by the path o f meditation and the seeds of the 108 knowledge obscurations 
that are objects to be abandoned by the path of meditation are progressively abandoned by 
the first ground included within the path of meditation until the tenth ground. [Haribhadra’s] 
Great Comm entary  explains:  
 
The clear realization of the truths is the same; therefore the first instant of the 
first ground is the path of seeing. Those other than  that, the second instant and so 
forth until the uninterrupted path called vajra -like, is the path of meditation.  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B-4. Mode of transference  
Know that as long as one has not completed the individual qualities of the training s, one 
will be on that ground; once they are completed one will transfer to another ground.  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects

The [Sanskrit] equivalent of “ground” is “bhumi.”111 When explained by way of definitive 
words that compound the syllables, it is as follows. It causes fearlessness to innumerable 
bhūtas112, or innumerable excellent qualities increase more and m ore, whereby it is called 
“ground.”

EXPLANATION  
By means of ten types of training s, qualities that act to train, the first ground will be 
attained. “Attitude,…” The way in which one trains is by conjoining these ten qualities 
with great compassion and the wisdom realizing the nature  of true existence as not 
observable . Sutra says:  
 
That which is the training  in the attitude of abiding on the first ground… acting 
in the way propounded; this is the training  in the words of truth.  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2A -1B. Commentary  
COMMENTARY 
So the ro ot text is said. Exactly in accordance with the sequence, (1) an attitude without 
treachery  regarding all bases, (2) benefit for one's own and others' purposes, (3) oneness of 
mind toward all sentient beings, (4) completely giving away all possessions, (5) pleasing the 
spiritual guide, (6) to desire an object of observation of the excellent dharma included 
within  the three vehicles, (7) strongly displeased to dwell in a household, (8) to desire the 
body of an unsurpassable buddha, (9) to clearly teach the e xcellent dharma, and (10) to only 
propound the truth —because it is an object to be generated in all ways through ten qualities 
with characteristics like the aforesaid, by only those training s with the characteristic of 
being  distinguished by not observing entitiness, the causal attributes of the training s, the 
first ground: Very Joyous, is attained.   
EXPLANATION  
The explanation exactly in accordance with the sequence  in the root text is as follows. 
The subject —the excellent quality in the continuum of a fi rst-grounder who is enthusiastic 
about upholding the excellent Dharma through (1) an  attitude without treachery such as 
deceit and dissimulation regarding all bases  such as sentient beings and roots of virtue —is 
a training  of the first ground , because it i s an excellent quality in the continuum of a first -
grounder that destroys its discordant classes and completes the excellent qualities. Apply 
similarly to the rest.  
 (2) They perform others’ welfare as much as possible, upholding the Mahayana dharma, 
bene fit for one's own and others' purposes, (3) special attitude of others’ welfare by way 
of one-pointedness of the mind  of the four immeasurables  toward all sentient beings, (4) 
completely giving away  all possessions such as the body, resources, roots of virtue, and so 
forth, and not generating even the slightest grasping of miserliness, (5) with the three doors 
pleasing the Mahayana spiritual guide, the root of the path, (6) to desire an object of 
observation of the excellent dharma included within the three  vehicles, observing the 
very extensive scriptural baskets, (7) due to seeing the drawbacks of being a householder, 
strongly displeased to dwell in a household, (8)  without being separated from the 
recollection of the buddha, to desire the body of an unsur passable buddha, (9) due to 
being without stinginess and miserliness of a master regarding the dharma, to clearly teach 
the excellent dharma, and (10) due to fulfilling one’s promise, to only propound the 
truth —through ten qualities of characteristic like the aforesaid, the first ground: Very 
Joyous, is attained, because it is an object to be generated  and attained in all ways since

1.34 Ethics, repaying that done, patience,  
Very joyful, great compassionate kindness ,

EXPLANATION  
One is able to train in the second ground by means of the eight, ethics and so forth, because one is able to destroy its discordant classes and complete the antidotes. On the second 
ground, not even the slightest stain of corrupte d ethics will arise in dreams. Since the 
perfection of patience and so forth are not possessed in as much a manner as the surpassing perfection of ethics, ethics is said to be “surpassing;” it is not that the remaining perfections 
do not exist on that grou nd. 
 Sutra says, “What is pure ethics ? Not paying attention to the mind of hearers and solitary 
realizers… [He] seeks the perfections in none other than [those]. This [bodhisattva] strives 
for and seeks the remaining perfections.”   
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2A -2B. Commentary  
COMMENTARY

sentient beings and of restraint, (2) to remember the benefit done by others, (3) to be patient 
towards the harms done by others and so forth,  (4) to not regret achieving virtuous qualities, 
(5) to love all beings, (6) to bow to the abbot and so forth, (7) to achieve the dharma taught by the spiritual guide, and (8) to be enthusiastic about the six perfections, generosity and so 
forth —the traini ngs like the aforesaid, by eight types of attributes that trained as before, the 
second ground: Stainless, is attained.  
 
EXPLANATION

welfare of sentient beings and the  ethics of restraint, (2) to remember the benefit done 
by others  and not allowing it to go to waste, a branch of purifying ethics , (3) to be patient 
towards the harms done by others and so forth, not thinking about the harms at all, 
possessing the patience of accepting suffering and definitely thinking about the dharma , (4) 
to be enthusiastic about  achieving virtuous qualities without regret, (5) to love all 
being s, never discarding sentient beings , (6) to bow to higher beings, the abbot and so 
forth, (7) to achieve the dharma taught by the spiritual guide  exactly as taught, and (8) 
to be enthusiastic about the six perfections, generosity and so forth , the excellen t

 The wrong view of deprecating actions and their results as well as its seeds have already 
been abandoned on the first ground. Therefore, [the wrong view (9) that is discussed here] is 
only in relation to the trainees who abide in the retinues [of bodhisattvas on this ground] at 
that time. Or alternatively, viewing an extreme that is held as a measure of the view holding 
to extremes is designated  a wrong view. When it is explained definitively to be a wrong 
view, you should take it that the antidote that casts to a distance114 has been generated. The 
other objects of behavior indicate d here will probably not manifest at this stage but are 
presented in order to make known that some of their seeds have been abandoned on this 
ground.

1.41 Undismayed when begged from,

EXPLANATION  
The sixth ground —on which the six excellent qualities “ generosity ” and so forth have been 
completed and the six faults have been abandoned —is perfectly attained, because it is 
attained by means of eliminating its faults and completing the excellent qualities. 
 Sutra says, “One who is abiding on the sixth ground should perfectly complete the six 
qualities, and in that way, the six perfections. Furthermore, he should completely abandon 
the six qualities. What are the six? He should completely abandon the mind of hearers and 
so forth.”

possessions voluntarily, and (12) the mind of a bandoning mendicants even though

214 The seventh ground is attained.  
 
EXPLANATION  
By severing the twenty faults  such as apprehending a self and sentient beings, the 
seventh ground is attained, because it is attained by means of completing twenty qualities 
of excellence . 
 Sutra says, “Twenty qualities do not occur in a great bodhisattva abiding on the s eventh 
ground. What are the twenty? They are apprehending a self, apprehending life, apprehending sentient beings...”  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2A -7A2. Commentary 
COMMENTARY 
So the root text is said. A dherence which views: —(1) self, ( 2) sentient beings, ( 3) life, (4) 
persons, (5) severance , (6) permanence , (7) signs, ( 8) causes, ( 9) aggregates, ( 10) 
constituents , (11) sources , (12-13) abidance in and attachment to  the three realms, ( 14) 
discourag ed mind, (15) buddha (16) dharma, (17) sangha, and ( 18) ethics —(19) dispute 
about emptiness and (20) holding to expressing contradictions —having separated from the 
twenty faults like the aforesaid  through full abandonment, by the twenty types of trainings , 
the implied  opposite qualiti es, as before, the seventh ground: Gone Afar, is fully known.  
 
EXPLANATION  
The subject —the seventh ground— has abandoned some seeds of apprehending (1) self, 
because it has cultivated for many eons the direct realization of the emptiness door of 
liberation. Apply similarly to the others; the y will also be explained on the occasion of the 
antidotes.  
  (19) Dispute about emptiness , holding emptiness as causing fear of things , and (20) 
holding to expressing the empty and the conventional as contradictions —having 
separated from the twenty faults like the aforesaid through full abandonment, by the 
twenty types of trainings , the implied twenty antidotes  that are  opposite qualities , that are 
the reverse qualities as before, the seventh ground: Gone Af ar, is fully known.

2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B. The tenth ground: the resultan t ground  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -1. Making a connection  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -2. Root text  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -3. Commentary  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -1. Making a connection  
COMMENTARY 
Having definitely indicated in that manner the training s of the causal grounds, on the 
resultant ground itself, without explaining those, by means of summarizing all the grounds, the characteristics of the tenth ground.  
 
EXPLANATION  
Having definitely indicated in that manner  explained above the training s of the causal 
grounds, the charac teristics of the tenth ground will be explained by means of 
summarizing the possession of the fully complete types of abandonments and realizations 
of all the  lower grounds . There is a reason for not explaining the tenth ground by means of 
the training s, because on the resultant tenth ground itself, without explaining those  
training s, one is able to completely understand them through just the explanation by means 
of the characteristics, whereby they are not explained .  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -2. Root text  
ROOT TEXT  
1.53 Having passed through the nine grounds, the exalted wisdom  
By which one abides on the buddha ground—  
Know that as  
The tenth bodhisattva ground.  
 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
220 EXPLANATION  
Know that. Know what? Know the exalted wisdom by which one abides on the buddha 
ground explained immediately after the explanation on the nine grounds, the objects to be 
passed through taught in sutra , having passed through the nine grounds —the eight lesser 
grounds, and the nine causal grounds, which have been taken as one, that called “the 
bodhisattva’s grounds” —the former by knowing and seeing, and the latter by abiding. 
Know that as what? Know that as  the tenth bodhisattva ground.  
 The subject —the buddha ground mentioned in sutra immediately after the nine grounds 
that are to be passed through—is the tenth ground, because it is a Mahayana ground and, in terms of one abiding on it, that a tenth -grounder “should be expressed as only a buddha who 
is a bodhisattva; he is not a perfect complete buddha” occurs. Sutra says: “Subhuti, who is a 
tenth-ground great bodhisattva? In this way, a great bodhisattva practices the six perfections 
with skillful means and ...,” “When he practices [those and so forth] until the eighteen 
unmixed qualities of a buddha, the ground of the lineage ...,” “He perfectly p asses through 
the bodhisattva grounds. Subhuti, in that way, having passed through the nine grounds, he 
abides on the buddha ground. Subhuti, that is a tenth -ground great bodhisattva.”  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -3. Commentary  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -3A. Explaining th e nine grounds that  are to be passed through  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -3B. Explaining the tenth ground that  has passed beyond those  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -3A. Explaining the nine grounds that  are to be passed through  
COMMENTARY 
So the root text is said. (1) The lineage of hearers and so forth, (2) approachers to the first result, (3) stream -enterers, (4) once -returners, (5) non -returners, (6) foe -destroyers 
themselves —the six aforesaid, and, (7) the three approachers of the remaining condensed, 
by intending to pr esent the three modes, and (8) solitary realizers , respectively are the 
grounds of (1) lineage, (2) the eighth, (3) seeing, (4) diminishment, (5) separation from attachment, (6) realizing completion, (7) hearers, and (8) solitary realizers; and (9) just one, 
the nine types of bodhisattva grounds which were explained.  
 
EXPLANATION  
(1) The subject —the four levels of the path of preparation that are based upon the lineage  
that is the dharmata of hearers , and solitary realizers [included within the expression]  “and 
so forth ”—are the grounds of lineage  respectively, because they are realizations of ordinary 
beings that are unmistaken regarding the lineage that have been correc tly adopted by the 
Hinayana. Persons who abide on the grounds of lineage are not certain  to be irreversible 
from their own enlightenment, just like the hearer who is someone fully transformed to the 
enlightenment of one abiding on a hearer’s path of preparation. It is not that it is not suitable 
to be posited by lineage, because [otherwise] i t follows that one does not realize the 
definition of the generality of a ground of lineage.   (2) The subject —the realizations of serialist approachers to the first result—are the 
grounds of the eighth , because they are realizations of the first component [of the first pair] 
from among the four pairs. (3)The realizations of the serialist stream -enterers  are the 
grounds of seeing , because they are realizations distinguished by having abandoned the 
three entwinements. (4) The subject —the realizations of once -returners —are the grounds 
of diminishment , because they are realizations of an abider in the result distinguished by 
having largely separated from attachment. (5) The realizations of non -returners  are the 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
221 grounds of  separation from attachment, because they are realizations of an abider in the 
result distinguished by having abandoned the partial concordances with the bottom. (6) The 
realizations of the hearer foe-destroyers themselves are the grounds of realizing 
completion, because they are realizations of the small vehicle distinguished by having 
abandoned the partial concordances with the upper. (7) The subject —the realizations of the 
three approachers  to the remaining results of a hearer—are the groun ds of  hearers,  
because they are realizations of the remaining approachers to the results of hearers.  
 There is a reason for condensing [these three] into one, known as “the grounds of 
hearers” here, because, by presenting the three modes  of a vehicle that are to be passed 
through having been viewed and to be passed through having been abided upon, it is 
intended to explain that having passed beyond them is a characteristic of the tenth ground, 
whereby they are condensed  into one, “the grounds of  hearers.”  
 (8) The subject —the clear realizations of solitary realizer  aryas —are the grounds of  
solitary realizers, because they are realizations of aryas of the middling vehicle. (9) The 
nine types of bodhisattva grounds which were explained earlier are  just one  of the nine 
grounds that are to be passed through, called “the grounds of bodhisattvas ,” because they 
are posited by condensing into one the grounds (that are passed through having been abided 
upon) which are the nine grounds to be passed through.  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -2B2B -3B. Explaining the tenth ground that  has passed beyond those  
COMMENTARY 
Having passed through the nine grounds in that manner, because that a tenth -grounder 
“should be expressed as only a buddha who is a bodhisattva; he is not a perfect complete 
buddha” occurs, one abiding on the buddha ground who knows prayers  is to be known as a 
tenth -grounder bodhisattva. 
 
EXPLANATION  
Immediately after indicating having passed through the nine grounds in that manner  in 
sutra, that explained to be one abiding on the buddha ground who knows prayers  with 
mastery over the actions of great clairvoyance, immediately after which is the buddha 
ground,  is to be known as a tenth- grounder bodhisattva,  because in terms of a 
bodhisattva abiding on that ground, that a tenth -grounder “should be expressed  as only a 
buddha who is a  bodhisattva; he is not a perfect complete buddha” occurs  in sutra. 
[Maitreya’s] Mahayana Treatise on the Sublime Continuum ( Mahayanottaratantrasastra ) 
[1.76] says:  
 This mode of the bodh isattvas  
During subsequent attainment [is seen] In the world to be just equal with the tathagatas  
With respect to correctly liberating sentient beings.  
 
Having pacified coarse exertion in enacting the welfare of others, they are taught to be equal to the buddhas in just engaging in exalted activities for all sentient beings, whereby they are 
described  as buddhas. However, these bodhisattvas have not exhaustively abandoned the 
levels of imprints of ignorance, the uncontaminated actions arisen from the ela borations of 
signs, a body that is the nature of mind, and inconceivable transformed death -transference. 
Hence they have not attained the truth body ’s perfection s of purity, bliss, an excellent self, 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
222 and permanen ce, thereby they are not referred to as “com plete buddhas.”115 “Prayer” refers 
to attaining the special ten powers116; when the actualizing of the truth body is asserted, it 
refers to the attainment of mastery.

EXPLANATION  
With regard to the accumulation of antidotes, an interceding stanza:   
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -3B. Root text  
ROOT TEXT  
1.54 Regarding the paths of seeing and familiarization,  
Because of thoroughly pacifying  
The conceptualizations of apprehendeds and apprehenders,  
They are said to be the eight types of antidotes. 
 
EXPLANATION  
The subject —regarding the Mahayana paths of seeing and familiarization —eight types 
of antidotes are said to  exist, because  there are eight by way of enumeration of acting to 
thoroughly pacify the  eight conceptualizations of apprehendeds and apprehenders , the 
objects of abandonment. 
 Sutra says, “Subhuti, from where will the vehicles arise?’ He said, ‘They will arise from 
the three realms by way of the mode of non -observation, and by way of the mode of non -
duality one will abide in omniscience wherever it is.”  
2B2C -1A2C -4B3B -3C. Commentary  
COMMENTARY 
So the root text is said. Conceptualizations of apprehendeds are two: those bearing the 
supports of (1) mere thoroughly afflicted things, and (2) an tidotes; and conceptualizations of 
apprehenders are two: those bearing the supports of (1) substantially existent persons and 
(2) imputed beings. Because of thoroughly pacifying the eight conceptualizations of 
apprehendeds and apprehenders, since they are individually abandoned on the paths of 
seeing and meditation, those to be actualized in dependence on aspects of both the two 
truths, by way of classification of discordant classes, the distinctions of the occasions of 
only the two paths, are eight types o f antidotes. 
 
                                              
115 Refer to [Maitreya’s] Mahayana Treatise on the Sublime Continuum (Mahayanottaratantrasastra ), 
stanzas 34 –36. 
116 The ten powers are: power s over (a) life, (b) mind, (c) necessities, (d) actions, (e) rebirth, (f) imagination, 
(g) wishes, (h) magical emanation, (i) exalted wisdom, and (j) Dharma. See the explanation of root text 
stanza 8.4.  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence        Chapter 1: The Exalted Knower of All Aspects  
 
223 EXPLANATION  
The subject —the distinctions  of the occasions that are able to eliminate the objects of 
abandonment of only the two, Mahayana paths  of seeing and meditation —are of eight 
types of antidotes, because there are eight by way of classification of discordant classes , 
the objects of abandonment. There are divisions of the discordant classes into eight, because 
they thoroughly pacify the eight conceptualizations of apprehendeds and apprehenders  
included within the acquired and the in nate.  
 The antidotes to the objects to be abandoned by the path of seeing and the objects to be 
abandoned by the path of meditation are indicated in sutra  to be actualized , that is, to be 
known  in dependence on aspects of both the two truths, because (i) i t has been taught 
that asserting the locus from where  one definite emerges and that which acts to definitely 
emerge as existing conventionally is  are the antidote s to the [ conceptions of] apprehendeds  
and apprehenders , the objects to be abandoned by the pa th of seeing, in dependence on 
conventional truths, and (ii) it has been taught that refuting them as existing ultimately is to 
be known as the antidote to the [conceptions of] apprehendeds  and apprehenders , the objects 
to be abandoned by the path of medit ation, in dependence on ultimate truths. It is 
appropriate to divide the antidotes into eight types by way of divisions of the discordant 
classes, since the seeds of the acquired and the seeds of the innate of the two conceptions of 
apprehendeds (called th e conceptualizations of apprehendeds) and the two conceptions of 
apprehenders are  individually abandoned on the paths of seeing and meditation  
respectively.

2B2C -1A2C -4B4C. Commentary  
COMMENTARY 
So the root text is said. (1) The objects of intent which have been  explained, (2) the equality 
of all phenomena, ( 3) to constantly en act the welfare of sentient beings, ( 4) through just  
performing all without signs, spontaneous  accomplishment , (5) attribute of the occasions  of 
the entity that is free from permanence  and severance , (6) to attain all aims of the three 
vehicles,  
 
EXPLANATION  
The achieving of definite emergence is of eight types , since all  the loci of  definite 
emerge nce are realized as being distinguished by just not being observed  ultimately, and 
conventionally the objects of definite  emerge nce are definite as eight types. The loci of  
definite emerge nce are definite as eight, because there are eight and there is no other 
phenomenon that is superior to such eight bases  of definite emerge nce. [The parts in 
bold occur in the commentary below.]  
 What are the eight?  (1) The three great objects of intent which have been explained, 
and are explained as the attributes of the achievings [by] the great Lotsawa, (2 -3) 
distinguish ed by seeing the equality of all phenomena  in being empty of true existence, to 
constantly , that is, infinitely  enact the welfare of sentient beings, by means of the great 
mind —that is, the great exalted compassion that knows the suffering of all sentient beings 
to be eliminated as equal —and application, (4) its attribute, as taught in [Maitreya’s] 
Mahayana Treatise on the Sublime Continuum ( Mahayanottaratantrasastra), “Indra, drum, 
cloud, Brahma...,” through just performing all without signs  of conception and exertion , 
spontaneous accomplishment  of others’ welfare.117 These three [(2)- (4)] are the attributes 
of the great mind. (5) Attaining exhaustion of the  objects of abandonment, attributes of the 
occasions of the entity that is free from permanence and sev erance  of the conception of 
extremes, due to having abandoned the extremes of cyclic existence and solitary peace , and 
(6) due to having exhausted the objects to be abandoned, attaining all aims that are objects 
of realization of the three vehicles —these two [(5) & (6)] are the attribute of great 
abandonment.

The explanation of the commentary on the first chapter from  the Ornament of the

 In this context [Haribhadra’s] Great Commentary says: 
 
The nature of the generation of the mind of only the very knower of all aspects and so forth, the aspects of practice due to establishing a relationship with the 
tathagatas, has been explained repeatedly by means of the nature of cause and 
result. Thus it is a chapter of this text. 
 Hence the mode in which the object -possessor is characterized by the objects is clearly 
indicated. The exalted knower of all aspects that is the object of seeking is expressed repeatedly by way of the ten topics that characterize an exalted knower of all aspects, 
stating: having generated the first mind [of enlightenment], one directly experiences the 
twenty -two generated minds progressively; likew ise, having directly experienced the four 
achievings progressively, one abides in the direct nature of all the ten topics in the present. Due to [this explanation of the first chapter] may the precious teachings spread and flourish 
in all directions throug h every means and remain for a long time.

COMMENTARY 
Without fully knowing the knower of paths, there is no realization of the exalted knower of all aspects. Hence the knower of paths:  
 
EXPLANATION  
Without completely cultivating and fully knowing the  knower of paths which proceeds to 
the direct realization  and attainment of the  exalted knower of all aspects, there is no  
attainment of an exalted  knower of all aspects. Hence , through inducing a mere cause from 
those [points] explained above, the knower of paths will be explained. [Haribhadra’s] 
Great Commentary says:  
QUALM: If it were like that —since the grounds, Very Joyous and so forth have 
been mentioned there [in the first chapter], the bodhisattva’s paths of seeing and 
so forth have been indicated —therefore why are they indicated again?  
RESPONSE: They are to be described; in order to eliminate the doubt that 
thinks, “The exalted knower of al l aspects of the tathagatas based  on the 
realization of all causes and results without exception was described [in the first chapter], whereby the paths of the v ictors ’ children have not been taught  in that 
[first chapter],” through drawing out  just the  causes in that [first chapter] itself, 
the knower of paths bearing the nature of the bodhisattva’s paths of seeing and so 
forth is separately posited.  
 Its meaning is as follows. There is a wrong conception that thinks:  
 
Although the paths of seeing and meditation included within the knower of paths 
have been indicated previously, when indicating that ‘The direct realization of 
the ten topics is the exalted knower of all aspects which is the object of seeking,’ [it has bee n taught that] all phenomena are directly seen by an exalted knower of 
all aspects. In order to attain that, it is sufficient to meditate solely on the 
                                             
 
118 If ‘grel  were emended to read ‘brel , this outline would read “ Making a connection by stating the  
relationship,” which seems to make more sense.  
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence                Chapter 2: The Knower of Paths  
 
227 meaning of the mode of abidance; there is no need to meditate on the complete 
paths of the three vehicle s. 
 Another thinks that:  
 
Omniscience will be attained even by merely going for refuge to the Three 
Jewels.  
 
Yet another wrong conception regarding the path of an exalted knower of all aspects thinks:  
 
Cultivating disillusionment by cyclic existence and despondency towards cyclic existence is wonderful for hearers and solitary realizers; since bodhisattvas need to remain in cyclic existence for others’ welfare, it is not like that for them.  
 For the sake of refuting these, just the  cause s from among the t en topics explained above are 
singled out. Hence a chapter is set out separately for the purpose of making known that, when those who wish to attain omniscience cultivate these complete three paths, they enter 
the ranks of “a knower of paths that proceeds to omniscience”; besides that, the paths will 
not occur from something trifling, and also the result from something trifling. Nowadays 
there are people who are like that, for they claim to practice the Mahayana path, yet seem 
not to know even the mere stru cture  of the common path.  
 The meaning of that statement is as follows. There is a reason for explaining the knower 
of paths after the exalted knower of all aspects, because it is for the sake of making known 
that one must cultivate the complete paths in o rder to attain it.

2B2C -1B2A -2. Commentary  
2B2C -1B2A -1. Root text  
ROOT TEXT  
2.1 In order to make the gods suitable, Eclipsing with light;  
Definite object; pervaded; 
Nature; and its action. 
 
EXPLANATION  
There is a purpose for eclipsing the lights of the gods  with the natural light of the tathagatas 
in order to make the gods of the desire and form realms  suitable  vessels for the generated 
mind [of enlightenment] by destroying their pride, because it is for the sake of making known that, in order to generate the gene rated mind and a knower of paths, the pride that 
does not seek higher attributes must be destroyed. Sutra says, “In the presence of the natural light of the tathagatas the light of the gods that arises from the maturation of karma is not 
vivid; it is not b right; the light of the tathagatas alone is the best,” and “Venerable Subhuti, 
The Explanation: Ornament of the Essence                Chapter 2: The Knower of Paths  
 
228 how do the bodhisattva mahasattvas abide in the Perfection of Wisdom? What is the 
Perfection of Wisdom of the bodhisattva mahasattvas?”  
 The object  that is the support for gene rating the knower of paths is definite , because in 
order to be skilled in the path that cultivates the complete three paths , prior to that  one 
definitely need s the support that is the desire to attain buddhahood for the sake of all three 
lineage -bearers. S utra says, “However, if they were to generate the mind [aspiring] for 
unsurpassable enlightenment, one should rejoice in them.”  
 It is not that it is unsuitable for those other than bodhisattvas to generate a knower of 
paths, because all sentient beings are pervaded  by the buddha lineage. The sutra related to 
this says: “Sons of the gods, even those who have not generated their mind [aspiring] for 
unsurpassable perfect complete enlightenment will also generate the mind [aspiring] for 
unsurpassable perfect c omplete enlightenment. Those who have entered the perfect 
faultlessness lack the might to generate the mind towards unsurpassable perfect complete 
enlightenment. Why? Because they have severed the boundaries of the continuum of cyclic 
existence.”  
 The mean ing of that is as follows. Those who have entered faultlessness are those on the 
hearer’s path of seeing. They do not take rebirth in cyclic existence even through the force 
of compassion and prayers. Having intentionally given up rebirth by way of karma a nd 
afflictions and put a stop to it, they have severed the continuity of rebirth in cyclic existence.  The purpose of mentioning this is for the sake of (i) causing those lineage -bearers 
indefinite in the hearers [lineage] to enter the Mahayana and (ii) ta king care of those who 
have already entered [the Mahayana]. In relation to this, [Haribhadra’s]  Great Commentary  
says:  
 
The protector Nagarjuna and his followers who propound one final vehicle assert that this sutra bears an intention, while the Acarya As anga and his followers who 
propound three final vehicles assert that this sutra is literally acceptable. 
Regarding the two systems that have been set forth, Acarya Asanga is asserted to 
have opened the chariot tracks of the Proponents of Cognition -Only; it  is not that 
Acarya Asanga himself is asserted to be a Proponent of Mind -Only . For, in 
Acarya Asanga’s commentary on the meaning of [Maitreya’s]  Mahayana 
Treatise on the Sublime Continuum (Mahayanottaratantrasastra) , Asanga 
proved that there is one final vehicle and also clearly commentated on the view 
of emptiness as the system of Middle -Way, differently from how it has occurred 
in [his] Summary of the Great Vehicle ( Mahayanasamgraha) and [his] Treatises 
on the Grounds; in this way even the Acarya [Haribhadra] is asserted to [have] 
the intention of Arya Asanga.  
 A knower of paths is superior in nature to hearers’ paths, because it bears the nature  of [the 
afflictions ] acting as a branch that accomplishes others’ welfare without principally 
abandoning the afflictions. Sutra says, “For me, it does not act as interference to the class of 
roots of virtue of those.”  
 Its actions  are also superior to those of hearers, because it  does not actualize the perfect 
end at an imp roper time and it  bears  the function of ripening and liberating infinite sentient 
beings. Sutra says, “[ It] shall attain qualities that are greatly superior to the superior 
qualities.”

COMMENTARY 
So the root text is said. In order to achieve the suitability to generate the knower of paths, 
eclipsing the maturation light of the gods and so forth by the natural lights of the tathagatas 
was done in order to make known that realizations are generated only in continua where 
pride has been destroyed. Because of that, the support is alluded to by words.  
 
EXPLANATION  
The subject —eclipsing the  contaminated light of maturation  of karma of the gods and so 
forth by the natural lights of the tathagatas —has a purpose, because it wa s done in 
order to achieve the suitability to generate  the generated mind [of enlightenment] and the 
knower of paths  through having destroyed strong pride, and in order to make known that 
realizations  of the generated mind [of enlightenment] and a knower o f paths are generated 
only in continua where  strong pride has been destroyed . The support  for generating the 
generated mind [of enlightenment] and a knower of paths is alluded to by words  in sutra, 
because it has been indicated that they are generated only  in continua where pride has been 
destroyed.  
2B2C -1B2A -2B. Cooperative condition  
COMMENTARY 
Also, since it is only one who has generated the mind towards enlightenment, it is definite to 
the individual object.  
 
EXPLANATION  
The subject—also  that knower of paths —is definite to the individual object that is the 
support who actually generates it , since that person who generates it without being 
obstructed by other paths  is only one who has generated the mind towards 
enlightenment.   
 Furthermore, fa miliarizing oneself with directly knowing the paths of all the three 
lineage -bearers is asserted to be enacting the welfare of the three lineage -bearers. Since no 
one other than a buddha is able to complete their welfare, it is clear that, having been 
preceded by the desire to attain buddhahood, [one has] to meditate on the three paths as a 
method [to achieve that]. Otherwise, it is clear that one would not be skilled in the divisions of how to practice the path.  
 In this way, through having indicated that  even beginners should know all three paths if 
they wish to quickly attain omniscience, it is made known that a generated mind is necessary in the support of a knower of paths.
